text: The city at night is a different animal. Neon signs cut through dark air, reflecting off wet pavement in fractured color. Traffic thins but never stops entirely. Windows glow amber and blue, each one a small world sealed off from the others. The streets belong to different people now: delivery workers, night-shift nurses, insomniacs walking without destination. Sound carries differently after midnight. A single car passing three blocks away is clearly audible. The city does not sleep so much as shift registers, trading one rhythm for another. It is quieter, sharper, more honest somehow. The daytime performance is over. What remains is the actual structure of the place, bare and functional and lit from within.
